finding: a stimulus attribute and an existing strength interacted conditionally on task structure; with one structure, additivity held across mean and distribution, whereas with an alternative structure, opposing interactive effects in the distribution produced mean additivity.
a learned predictive association between a stimulus and response attenuated a semantic-conflict effect more than did attention-modulation based on stimulus identity.
repetition increased the probability of replicating prior responses (correct or incorrect), revealing implicit learning of both accurate and erroneous response patterns.

practicing one operation (multiplication or factoring) produced interference with the other, but practicing both produced bidirectional facilitation, showing that memory specializes under unidirectional practice but maintains flexibility under bidirectional practice.
goal primes (action vs. rest) increased or decreased both motor and cognitive output nonselectively, and this output effect reversed once the activated goal state was attained.
a trait disposition toward disgust predicted implicit and spontaneous (but not explicit) disapproval of a social target.
visual perspective (first-person vs. third-person viewpoint) bidirectionally influenced the abstractness with which an action was identified.

language-background groups differed in initial reading achievement and growth rate; these differences substantially reduced when socioeconomic status was explicitly modeled, suggesting the language effect was mediated or confounded by social class.

a stimulus property (autobiographical salience, arousal, familiarity) evoked nostalgia, moderated by trait characteristics (nostalgia proneness, mood, personality); the trait itself was predicted by neuroticism-related dimensions.
demand on working-memory maintenance and distractor-interference triggered dynamic adjustments in cognitive control, improving subsequent performance in the same manner as conflict-task demands.

a visual stimulus attribute (red color) increased attraction and desirability in a romantic context, mediated by perceived status, with the effect specific to romantic (not general social) evaluation.
a lower social-class position, despite fewer resources, predicted greater prosocial behavior, mediated by egalitarian values and compassion.
an identical transgression was judged more severely when located in the future than in the equidistant past, driven by stronger emotional reactions to future events.

responses obtained before a stimulus event (action, priming, recall) showed statistically significant effects in the direction of the stimulus, suggesting anomalous retroactive influence; stimulus-seeking personality moderated the effect size.

chronic social disconnection predicted increased physical-warmth behavior (bathing), and physical-warmth interventions reduced affiliation-seeking and loneliness, indicating physical warmth substitutes for social warmth in self-regulation outside explicit awareness.
thinking that involved associative progression (moving across concepts) produced better mood than thinking restricted to a single concept.
a hierarchical (asymmetric) social relationship was processed faster and learned more readily than non-hierarchical alternatives, and this processing ease increased preference for the hierarchy.

focusing attention on physical body characteristics reduced attributed agency (self-control) but increased attributed experience (emotion and sensation), redistributing rather than removing perceived mind.
experiential (vs. material) purchases were more closely associated with self-identity, and this self-centrality mediated greater satisfaction from experiences than possessions.

harboring a burdensome secret altered perception (hills steeper, distances farther, tasks more effortful) and behavior (less helping), proportional to secret burden, analogous to carrying physical weight.
